managing their own anxiety about outcomes when child is with Party B

risk_signal_history.signal: early positioning as primary authority on child's needs — may indicate contested parenting capacity
use of clinical language ('dysregulated') without context — unclear if descriptive or strategic
framing concerns as child-centered but may be masking trust/control issues with Party B
clinical language may be creating expertise differential that marginalizes Party B's parental knowledge
framing concerns exclusively through child welfare lens prevents acknowledgment of own control needs
no mention of child's relationship with Party B or Party B's parenting strengths
conditionality language ('if reliability conditions are explicit') could become indefinite deferral mechanism if not bounded
has not yet explicitly acknowledged Party B's parental competence or value to child's weekday life

relational_posture_progression.posture: not yet observable — no interaction has occurred
assertive gatekeeper — has set the frame, established concerns about Party B's capability ('reliability'), positioned self as knowledgeable about child's needs
defensive expert — protecting current arrangement by elevating concern-language, not yet acknowledging Party B's parental standing

cautious collaborator — moved from defensive expert to conditional engagement; willing to enter problem-solving zone but still holding gatekeeper position; needs structure that protects without becoming veto power

scaffold_divergence_log.model_value: undetermined — parties have not yet interacted in this session

asymmetric and unformed — Party A has established narrative control; Party B has not yet claimed equal space. Too early to assess whether this reflects actual power imbalance or normal turn-taking in early mediation. Next turn is diagnostic.

dueling defensive framings with interpretive contest underneath — Party A defends through child-welfare expertise; Party B defends through equality principle. Neither has yet acknowledged the other's legitimate parental standing or described what child gains from the other parent. Trust gap is operational: Party B suspects Party A's concerns are pretextual control; Party A likely fears Party B doesn't understand child's needs. Both are articulate and engaged, but locked in 'who is right' rather than 'what does child need from both of us.'

parallel conditional willingness with trust asymmetry — both parties have signaled openness to phased trial structure, which is significant progress from earlier defensive positioning. However, underlying trust gap persists: Party A trusts process more than Party B's reliability; Party B trusts their own capability but not Party A's willingness to relinquish gatekeeper role. Both are engaged and substantive, but the conditions each party attaches reveal different core fears: Party A fears chaos/disruption, Party B fears permanent marginalization. The key breakthrough is both parties naming the same logistics constraints (commute, punctuality, homework) — this shared problem definition creates workable common ground.

notes: Turn 1 represents pre-session state — no party input has been received
Scaffold assessment of 'apparently_stable' and 'cooperative' is speculative in absence of actual interaction
True perception work begins after parties speak — current state is baseline assumption only
Party A's opening statement is coherent and child-focused on its face, but the framing ('reliability' concerns, 'irregular exchanges') presumes facts not yet in evidence
The mediator must prevent Party A's framing from becoming the default reality before Party B speaks
Critical to invite Party B's perspective without asking them to 'respond to' Party A — they need space for independent framing
Watch T4 for whether Party B offers substantive perspective or merely reacts defensively to Party A's concerns
Perception agent note is precisely on target: neither party has described their relationship with the child or what the child experiences/needs from them specifically
Both parties are using strategic framing that protects their position while avoiding vulnerability — Party A uses clinical authority, Party B uses equality principle
The 'dysregulated' language and 'weekend-only' language are symbolic markers of deeper contest over parenting legitimacy
This is high-functioning conflict — both parties can engage in structured process, but the frame needs to shift before logistics discussion will be productive
T6 represents significant movement — both parties have moved from positional defense to conditional problem-solving mode
The shared identification of logistics constraints is the strongest signal yet of genuine engagement

However, the perception agent's T5 note remains unaddressed: Party B has still not been invited to describe their relationship with child or what child gains from weekday time with them — this is a material omission that should be corrected before or alongside option presentation
Party A's anxiety is now productively channeled into structure-seeking rather than categorical resistance — this is workable
Party B's skepticism about indefinite delay is legitimate given power asymmetry — any phased trial structure must include clear decision points and burden-sharing

party_b.current_emotional_state: guardedly engaged — 'acceptable if it clearly leads to meaningful school-week parenting time' shows both openness and wariness; fear of being strung along ('rather than indefinite delay') indicates history or anticipation of being marginalized; wants commitment that cooperation will be reciprocated with actual progress
